summary: Market sentiment is cautiously optimistic but fragile, with investors balancing geopolitical de-escalation signals against persistent macro uncertainties, as evidenced by flat European equities and a firming dollar weighing on gold, which slipped to a two-week low. The dominant macro driver is the evolving US-Iran nuclear framework deal, with IAEA inspections confirmed to proceed, ships resuming passage through the Strait of Hormuz under a UN evacuation scheme, and Trump confirming Iran is not seeking Hormuz tolls, collectively pushing Brent crude to its lowest level since before the Iran conflict began on expectations of smoother oil flows. On the sector and stock front, Qualcomm advanced after inking an AI deal with startup Modular to strengthen its software stack and data center capabilities, Rheinmetall slid as Iran tensions eased and defense demand expectations softened, while Cboe jumped into prediction markets to capitalize on zero-day options growth momentum. Key risks investors are monitoring include NATO alliance fractures highlighted by Italy rebuking Secretary General Rutte over US base access for an Iran war scenario, lingering Gulf ally skepticism about the Iran deal's durability, EU aviation warnings to avoid Iranian airspace, and uncertainty around Ukraine-Russia talks pending Witkoff and Kushner availability. In the short term, oil price trajectory, IAEA inspection progress, and Treasury Secretary Bessent's optimistic forecast of US GDP returning to 3% growth before year-end will be central catalysts, with markets likely to remain range-bound pending clearer confirmation that the Iran diplomatic framework holds.
